title: Overview
text: steps-widget traces how Python actually evaluates an expression, one substitution or reduction at a time. Tag any statement in a cell with a trailing # PRINT STEPS comment and the %%steps cell magic renders the evaluation trace as a widget below the cell – the same trace the print-steps command line tool prints to the terminal for a whole script.

title: Overview
text:
Installation

pixicondapip


pixi workspace channel add munch-group
pixi add steps-widget


conda install -c munch-group steps-widget


pip install steps-widget



steps-widget’s stepper dispatches on CPython 3.9/3.10 bytecode opcode names that Python 3.11 restructured, so it only installs on Python 3.9 or 3.10.

title: Overview
text:
The %%steps cell magic
Importing steps_widget registers the %%steps cell magic. The cell still runs normally; every statement tagged # PRINT STEPS also gets a trace card below the cell showing each substitution and reduction step.

import steps_widget


%%steps
x = 7
y = 5
z = x * y + 4  # PRINT STEPS
k = z * 42  # PRINT STEPS

You can also build a steps_widget.widget.StepsWidget directly from a source string, the same way the cell magic does internally:

from steps_widget import StepsWidget

StepsWidget('''
z = x * y + 4  # PRINT STEPS
''')

title: Overview
text:
The print-steps command line tool
Outside of a notebook, print-steps traces every # PRINT STEPS-tagged line of a student script and prints the trace to stderr:
print-steps student_script.py
It first runs the script unmodified to confirm it is error-free, then re-runs an instrumented copy so line numbers and execution order match the original file.

title: Function reference
text:
The %%steps cell magic and the StepsWidget it renders.



StepsWidget
Displays the substitution/reduction trace of # PRINT STEPS-tagged statements.


register_steps_magic
Register the %%steps cell magic.






The print-steps console script.



run_student_file
Trace every # PRINT STEPS-tagged line of a student script to stderr.

title: Examples
text:
A few more %%steps examples, showing method calls, indexing, and short-circuit and/or logic.
import steps_widget

title: Examples
text:
Method calls and indexing

%%steps
s = 'Hej {}'
l = [1, 2, 3]
greeting = s.format('Kasper').upper()[0:3]  # PRINT STEPS
total = l[0] + l[1] + l[2]  # PRINT STEPS

title: Examples
text:
Short-circuit and / or
Each Logic step shows which side of and/or Python actually needed to look at.

%%steps
x = 0
y = 3
result = x and y or 7  # PRINT STEPS
